content:
14 min read
Just now

--

“The build is still running.”
My developer said this like it was completely normal. Like waiting 45 minutes to find out if your code works was just part of the job.
I looked at our GitHub Actions dashboard. The pipeline had been running for 38 minutes. It had 7 more minutes to go.
We deployed twice a day. Sometimes three times. Each deployment cost us 45 minutes of waiting — plus the context-switching, the interrupted flow, the “let me check if the build passed” distraction loop.
I did the math.
3 deployments/day × 45 minutes × 5 developers = 11.25 hours of CI time daily.
That’s one full developer wasted. Every. Single. Day.
Not building features. Not fixing bugs. Waiting for a green checkmark.
Something had to change.
Over three months, I ripped the pipeline apart and rebuilt it from scratch. I applied every optimization I could find: caching, parallelization, test splitting, skipping unnecessary steps, smarter Docker builds.
The result: 45 minutes → 4 minutes. An 11x improvement.
3 deployments/day × 4 minutes × 5 developers = 1 hour of CI time daily.
We got 10+ hours of developer time back every single day.
Let me show you exactly what we did.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Generated by AuthorThe Original Pipeline (The Crime Scene)
Before I show you the solution, you need to understand how bad it was.
This was our original .github/workflows/deploy.yml:
name: Deploy
on:
push:
branches: [main]
jobs:
pipeline:
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with:
node-version: '20'
- name: Install dependencies
run: npm install # Not npm ci!
- name: Lint
run: npm run lint
- name: Type check
run: npm run type-check
- name: Unit tests
run: npm run test
- name: Build
run: npm run build
- name: E2E tests
run: npm run test:e2e
- name: Build Docker image
run: docker build -t myapp:latest .
- name: Push to registry
run: docker push myapp:latest
- name: Deploy to production
run: ./scripts/deploy.sh
A single job. Every step runs sequentially. No caching. No parallelization. No skipping anything.
Here’s where the 45 minutes was actually going:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every step waited for the previous one. Even things that had nothing to do with each other — like linting and type-checking — ran one after another.
And the worst part? This ran on every push to main. Even commits that only changed a README.
The Optimization Playbook
I approached this systematically. Not randomly tweaking things and hoping for speedups. A structured approach:

• Profile first — find where the time actually goes

• Parallelize — run independent things simultaneously

• Cache aggressively — never redo work you’ve already done

• Be smart about what you run — skip unnecessary work
Let’s go through each.
Optimization 1: Caching Dependencies (The Quickest Win)
Time saved: 4 minutes 20 seconds
The very first step in our pipeline: npm install. Every single build. 4 minutes and 20 seconds of downloading the exact same packages every time.
This is the most common CI waste I see. You’re not changing dependencies on every commit. node_modules can be cached.
Before:
- name: Install dependencies
run: npm installAfter:
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with:
node-version: '20'
cache: 'npm' # Built-in npm caching in setup-node!
- name: Install dependencies
run: npm ci # Use npm ci, not npm installTwo changes here:

• cache: 'npm' in setup-node automatically caches ~/.npm keyed by your package-lock.json. If package-lock.json hasn't changed, you get a cache hit and skip the download.

• npm ci instead of npm install. npm ci is faster, more reliable, and reproducible — it installs exactly what's in package-lock.json without resolving. Use npm install in development, npm ci in CI.
Result on cache hit: npm install in 14 seconds instead of 4 minutes 20 seconds.
For manual cache control with more flexibility:
- name: Cache node_modules
uses: actions/cache@v4
with:
path: ~/.npm
key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
restore-keys: |
${{ runner.os }}-node-The key uses a hash of package-lock.json. Change a dependency → new cache key → fresh install. Same dependencies → cache hit → 14 seconds.
Time after this optimization: ~44 minutes → ~40 minutes
Good start. But we needed bigger wins.
Optimization 2: Parallelizing Jobs (The Biggest Win)
Time saved: ~22 minutes
Look at our original pipeline again. These steps had zero dependencies on each other:

• Lint

• Type check

• Unit tests
All three could run simultaneously. Instead, they ran one after another, adding 18+ minutes sequentially.
This is like having three developers work on three independent tasks, but making them take turns instead of working in parallel.
The fix: split into parallel jobs.
name: Deploy
on:
push:
branches: [main]
jobs:
# These three run SIMULTANEOUSLY
lint:
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with:
node-version: '20'
cache: 'npm'
- run: npm ci
- run: npm run lint
type-check:
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with:
node-version: '20'
cache: 'npm'
- run: npm ci
- run: npm run type-check
unit-tests:
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with:
node-version: '20'
cache: 'npm'
- run: npm ci
- run: npm run test
# Build only runs after all three pass
build:
needs: [lint, type-check, unit-tests]
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with:
node-version: '20'
cache: 'npm'
- run: npm ci
- run: npm run buildneeds: [lint, type-check, unit-tests] means build only runs after all three parallel jobs succeed. If lint fails, the build doesn't even start.
What this looks like visually:
Before (sequential):
Lint (3m) → Type-check (7m) → Unit tests (9m) → Build (8m)
Total: 27 minutes just for these stepsAfter (parallel):
Lint (3m) ↘
Type-check (7m) → Build (8m) ← All three finish in 7 minutes
Unit tests (9m) ↗
Total: 15 minutes for these steps
The bottleneck becomes the slowest parallel job (type-check at 7 minutes), not the sum of all jobs.
Parallel jobs allow the user to run multiple tasks simultaneously, resulting in reduced overall build times and improved efficiency.
Time after this optimization: ~40 minutes → ~22 minutes
We were getting somewhere.
Optimization 3: Splitting E2E Tests (The Sneaky Time Killer)
Time saved: ~8 minutes
Our E2E tests (87 Playwright tests) took 11 minutes 20 seconds. Running sequentially.
The tests had no dependency on each other. Auth tests, dashboard tests, payment tests — all independent.
The fix: test sharding. Split 87 tests across multiple runners, running them simultaneously.
e2e-tests:
needs: [build]
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
strategy:
matrix:
shard: [1, 2, 3] # Split into 3 parallel shards
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with:
node-version: '20'
cache: 'npm'
- run: npm ci
- name: Run E2E tests (shard ${{ matrix.shard }}/3)
run: npx playwright test --shard=${{ matrix.shard }}/3
- uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
if: failure()
with:
name: playwright-report-${{ matrix.shard }}
path: playwright-report/Three runners. Each gets ~29 tests. Instead of 11 minutes 20 seconds sequential, we get ~4 minutes parallel (plus ~30 seconds overhead).
Time saved: ~7 minutes 30 seconds
Time after this optimization: ~22 minutes → ~15 minutes
Getting better. But we still had more to shave off.
Optimization 4: Smarter Docker Builds (The Hidden Time Sink)
Time saved: ~2 minutes 45 seconds
Our Dockerfile was naive:
# Before - terrible for caching
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY . . # Copy EVERYTHING first
RUN npm ci # Then install dependencies
RUN npm run build
CMD ["node", "server.js"]Every build copied everything (including source code), then installed dependencies. Since source code changes on every commit, npm ci ran fresh every single time. Docker layer cache: useless.
The fix: copy package.json first, install dependencies, then copy source.
# After - optimized layer ordering
FROM node:20-alpine AS base
WORKDIR /app
# Layer 1: Dependencies (cached unless package.json changes)
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production
# Layer 2: Source code (cache busted on every commit - expected)
COPY . .
RUN npm run build
# Layer 3: Production image
FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=base /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=base /app/node_modules ./node_modules
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]Now Docker caches the npm ci layer separately from source code. If package.json hasn't changed (90% of commits), Docker reuses the cached dependency layer. Only the source copy and build run fresh.
Before: Docker build ~3 minutes 15 seconds (no cache benefit) After: Docker build ~30 seconds (on cache hit)
Also added Docker layer caching in GitHub Actions:
- name: Build Docker image
uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
with:
context: .
push: true
tags: myapp:${{ github.sha }}
cache-from: type=gha # Use GitHub Actions cache
cache-to: type=gha,mode=max # Write back to cacheTime after this optimization: ~15 minutes → ~12 minutes
Optimization 5: Skip Unchanged Code (Run Less, Ship Faster)
Time saved: ~4 minutes (on qualifying PRs)
This was the most impactful mindset shift: not every commit needs to run every check.
A commit that changes README.md doesn't need to run E2E tests. A commit that changes a backend API doesn't need to rerun frontend E2E tests. A commit that only touches tests doesn't need a full build.
GitHub Actions has paths filtering:
name: Frontend CI
on:
push:
branches: [main]
paths:
- 'src/frontend/**'
- 'package.json'
- 'package-lock.json'
- '.github/workflows/frontend.yml'
jobs:
frontend-tests:
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- run: npm ci
- run: npm run test:frontendThis job only runs when frontend files change. If you push a backend-only change, the frontend job is skipped entirely.
For our monorepo, we split into focused workflows:
# frontend.yml - runs on frontend changes
paths:
- 'apps/web/**'
- 'packages/ui/**'
# backend.yml - runs on backend changes
paths:
- 'apps/api/**'
- 'packages/shared/**'
# infra.yml - runs on infra changes
paths:
- 'terraform/**'
- 'docker/**'The result: a backend fix only runs backend tests. A typo fix in docs runs nothing.
Skip unnecessary tests: if no relevant code has changed, skip unrelated tests.
This needs careful thought though — your paths must be comprehensive enough that you don't skip something critical. We had one incident where a shared package change wasn't in the frontend paths, so frontend tests didn't run, and we shipped a broken UI. Lesson: when in doubt, include the path.
Optimization 6: Build Artifacts Instead of Rebuilding (The Elegant Fix)
Time saved: ~7 minutes
We had this pattern:
job1: build → artifact
job2: build → artifact (again!)
job3: build → artifact (again!!)Multiple jobs building the same thing. Every job that needed the built output would rebuild it from scratch.
The fix: build once, share via artifacts.
build:
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with:
node-version: '20'
cache: 'npm'
- run: npm ci
- run: npm run build # Upload build artifacts
- uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
with:
name: build-output
path: .next/
retention-days: 1
e2e-tests:
needs: [build]
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
# Download instead of rebuild
- uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
with:
name: build-output
path: .next/
- name: Run E2E tests
run: npx playwright test
Build once. The E2E test job downloads the artifact instead of rebuilding. 7 minutes 30 seconds saved per job that would have rebuilt.
Optimization 7: Fail Fast (Don’t Waste Time on Doomed Builds)
Time saved: varies, but crucial for feedback loop
If lint fails, there’s no point running type-check, unit tests, and building. Fail fast. Give the developer feedback immediat
